root.title: Cúram 8.1.2 Nominees Guide
root.summary: Front matter and main overview for the Cúram 8.1.2 Nominees Guide. It covers what a nominee is for case payments and bills, including the primary client as the default nominee, alternative nominees, and eligible participant types. It explains maintaining nominee data such as creation, delivery patterns, addresses, bank accounts, activation/deactivation, and change history. It also describes assigning case components to nominees and how financial components are generated, issued, rolled up into payments/bills, and viewed. Additional topics include reassessment for over/underpayments, reissuing canceled payments, and reviewing nominee financial transactions. The section also contains the note, edition, contents, notices, privacy policy, and trademarks pages.

children.title: Note
children.summary: This section is a brief front-matter note telling readers to review the Notices section on page 19 before using the information or the supported product. It also includes the Merative US L.P. copyright notice for 2012 and 2024.

children.title: Edition
Edition — pages 5-14
children.summary: Front matter for the Cúram Nominees guide. States the edition applies to Cúram 8.1, 8.1.1, and 8.1.2, with Merative copyright/trademark notices. The contents shown indicate the chapter on “Cúram Nominees overview,” covering nominee roles and types, maintaining nominee information (creation, delivery patterns, addresses, bank accounts, deactivation/reactivation), assigning case components, and paying/billing nominees. This node is useful as the entry point for understanding nominee-related case payment and billing administration.

children.title: Edition — pages 15-18
children.summary: Explains how Cúram generates and processes nominee financial components, including delivery patterns, cover periods, and payment amounts. Covers examples for single and multiple nominees, multiple components with different delivery patterns, and how components roll into instruction line items, financial instructions, and recorded payment instruments. Also describes case reassessment handling over/underpayments, reissuing canceled payments to original or alternate nominees, and viewing nominee financial transactions in case financials and Participant Manager.

Front matter and chapter entry for the Cúram Nominees guide, valid for Cúram 8.1–8.1.2. Introduces nominee concepts for case payments/bills: who can be a nominee, primary client vs alternative nominee, case components, and maintaining nominee data (creation, delivery patterns, addresses, bank accounts, activation status). Also covers assigning components, generating financial components for one or multiple nominees, issuing payments/bills, reassessment over/underpayments, reissuing canceled payments, and viewing nominee financial transactions.

children.title: Notices
Privacy policy
children.summary: This section contains legal and copyright notices: fictitious sample data disclaimers, terms for using sample programs, warranty/liability limitations, the Merative privacy policy link, and trademark acknowledgments for Merative, IBM, Adobe, Oracle, Linux, Microsoft, UNIX, and others. It is useful for locating licensing, privacy, and branding/legal information.

children.title: Trademarks
children.summary: Lists trademark notices and legal ownership for Merative, IBM, Adobe, Oracle/Java, Linux, Microsoft, and UNIX. Also states that sample names in the document are fictitious, sample programs are provided “as is” under the Merative Client Agreement, and gives a link to the Merative privacy policy. Includes copyright notice for Merative US L.P. (2012, 2024).

Legal notices and usage terms for the publication: personal and enterprise reproduction limits, prohibition on derivative works or external redistribution without consent, and requirement to preserve proprietary notices. States no additional rights are granted, Merative may withdraw permission, and export laws apply. Includes strong warranty/liability disclaimers, patent notice, notes on non-Merative product and website references, and copyright © Merative US L.P. 2012, 2024.

children.title: Contents
children.summary: Table of contents for the Cúram Nominees guide. It lists front matter items (Note, Edition, Notices, Privacy policy, Trademarks) and the main chapter “Cúram Nominees overview,” with subsections on nominees, maintaining nominee information (creation, delivery patterns, addresses, bank accounts, deactivation/reactivation), assigning nominee case components, and paying/billing nominees (financial components, multiple nominees, multiple components, issuing/reissuing payments, case reassessment, transaction viewing). Includes page references for navigation.

children.title: 1 Cúram Nominees overview
1.1 Nominees
children.summary: Explains the nominee role in Cúram cases: the primary client is automatically the default nominee for payments and bills, but an alternative participant may receive them instead. Covers nominee types that can be assigned (household member, person, employer, representative) and how payments/bills are issued against case components tied to eligibility. Also notes case components may be financial or non-financial, and that the primary client cannot be changed after being set.

children.title: 1.2 Maintaining Nominee Information
Creating Nominees
children.summary: Explains how caseworkers create and maintain nominee records. Nominees can be added for case participants, registered persons, employers, or unregistered participants (creating a Representative participant). A nominee needs at least one delivery pattern, which determines how payments/bills are sent and may default from the product or primary client. The section also covers recording currency, preferred office, and relationship to the primary client, automatic creation of the nominee role, active status requirements, and changing delivery patterns for payment/billing preferences.

children.title: Maintaining Nominee Delivery Patterns
children.summary: This section explains how nominee delivery patterns are created and maintained. It defines a delivery pattern as the payment/billing method and frequency used for a nominee acting on behalf of the primary client, and notes that nominees can have multiple active patterns. It also covers changing patterns to suit payment or billing needs, and how delivery patterns tie to destination details like address or bank account. Useful for understanding nominee setup and payment/billing delivery options.

children.title: Maintaining Nominee Addresses and Bank Accounts
children.summary: Explains how nominee addresses and bank accounts are maintained so the organization can issue payments, bills, and EFT transfers. The nominee’s primary registered address is used by default for check, voucher, and invoice delivery, but users can change the destination by adding a new address or selecting another participant address. New addresses are added to the nominee’s participant address list, and modified addresses used by a nominee are tracked.

children.title: Deactivating and Reactivating Nominees
children.summary: This section explains how nominees can be deactivated and later reactivated. Deactivation changes a nominee’s status from Active to Inactive, preventing assignment to case components and ensuring payments/bills are not issued to an ineligible nominee. A primary client or any nominee actively receiving case components cannot be deactivated. Reactivation restores the nominee’s details on the case, makes them eligible again for assignment, and returns the status to Active.

Covers how caseworkers create and maintain nominee records, including automatic nominee creation for the primary client, adding nominees for participants/representatives, required delivery patterns, and recorded details such as currency, preferred office, and relationship. Explains managing delivery patterns over time, including multiple active patterns and change history. Describes maintaining nominee addresses and bank accounts for payment, bill, and EFT destinations, with defaults and update rules. Also covers deactivating inactive nominees and reactivating them when eligible again.

children.title: 1.3 Assigning Nominee Case Components
children.summary: Explains how case components are assigned to nominees and how payment/billing delivery patterns are set. Components start assigned to the primary client at case creation, but can later be reassigned to one or more nominees, with only one nominee per component at a time and non-overlapping assignment periods. Covers adding new nominees, using different delivery patterns for the same nominee, and special handling for once-off underpayment components. Also describes setting a default nominee, which receives any unassigned components.

children.title: 1.4 Paying and Billing Nominees
Generating Financial Components
children.summary: Explains how financial components are generated from case component assignments. A financial component is the payment or billing schedule used to deliver benefits or liabilities, created automatically when a case is activated and the primary client is eligible. The system uses component assignment information and the nominee’s delivery pattern to calculate the schedule period, method, and frequency (for example, weekly EFT payments). This section is useful for understanding how assignments become actual payments or bills.

children.title: Generating Financial Components for Multiple Nominees
children.summary: Explains how financial components are generated for multiple nominees on a case component. Components are created nominee-by-nominee and split by each nominee’s delivery pattern and assignment period. Example shows John Smith receiving weekly arrears payments from case start, then Linda Smith taking over later with a different delivery pattern, producing separate cover periods, amounts, and due dates. Also notes that each financial component includes nominee, primary client, amount, delivery method/frequency, and cover period, and that secondary components may be created for deductions or third-party payments.

children.title: Generating Financial Components for A Nominee Assigned to Multiple Components With Different Delivery Patterns
children.summary: Explains how financial components are generated for a nominee assigned multiple case components with different delivery patterns over the same period. Example: John Smith receives Income Assistance weekly by check in advance on Monday and Medical Assistance monthly by check in advance on the 1st, from case start Nov 1 to Dec 31. Shows resulting cover periods, amounts, and payment due dates for each component, including a partial final weekly cover period and a full monthly component.

children.title: Issuing Payments and Bills to Nominees
children.summary: Explains how Cúram automatically issues payments and bills to nominees during financial processing. Financial components are converted into financial instruction line items, then rolled up into financial instructions and finally generated as payments or bills. The section notes that instruction line items carry nominee, delivery pattern, amount, and coverage period details, and can consolidate across multiple cases. It also states that actual financial instruments are recorded when payments/bills are issued, and briefly introduces case reassessment, including overpayments and underpayments.

children.title: Case Reassessment
children.summary: Case reassessment re-evaluates case payments or bills when a change in circumstance is recorded. It can detect overpayments or underpayments and create the appropriate corrective case based on administration settings. Example: if a person paid $50 is later found eligible for $40, a $10 bill is generated to recover the overpayment.

children.title: Reissuing Payments to a Nominee
children.summary: Explains how underpayment rectifications and canceled payments are issued to nominees. By default, payments go to the original nominee, but an alternative nominee can receive an underpayment only if their case component assignment covers the full underpayment period; underpayments cannot be split across nominees. Reissued canceled payments can go to the original or an alternative nominee, with delivery pattern selection if needed, and only nominees already on the case are eligible. Also describes financial transaction records and viewing all nominee payments in Participant Manager.

children.title: Viewing Financial Transactions for Nominees
children.summary: Explains how financial transactions for nominees are recorded and viewed. Underpayments and canceled payments can be reissued to the original or an alternate nominee, but only if the nominee already exists on the case and, for underpayments, the alternate assignment covers the full cover period. The case financials list shows nominee name, amount, transaction type, and effective date. Also notes that Participant Manager provides an all-cases view of payment instruments issued to a person as nominee.

Explains how case component assignments are turned into financial processing for nominees. Covers automatic generation of financial components on case activation, using delivery patterns to set payment/billing schedules, cover periods, amounts, and frequency. Shows how one component can be split across multiple nominees or how one nominee can receive multiple components with different delivery patterns. Also covers conversion to financial instruction line items, rolling up to payments/bills, recording financial instruments, reassessment for over/underpayments, reissuing canceled or corrective payments, and viewing nominee transactions.

Overview of Cúram nominees and how they work in case payments/bills. Explains that the primary client is auto-created as the default nominee and cannot later be changed, while other participants, employers, or representatives can be assigned. Covers nominee setup and maintenance, including delivery patterns, addresses, bank accounts, activation/deactivation, and history of changes. Also explains assignment of case components to nominees and how they become financial instructions, payments/bills, including reassessment, under/overpayments, reissuing canceled payments, and viewing nominee transactions.
